walkthrough.title: Choose Your Player Count
walkthrough.text: Let's get Sheriff of Nottingham set up. First, figure out how many players you have. This game plays three to five. The player count affects two things: which cards stay in the deck and how many times each player will be Sheriff. We will adjust those in a moment.

walkthrough.title: Remove Cards for Three Players
walkthrough.text: If you have exactly three players, you need to thin the deck before shuffling. Find all cards with a four-plus icon printed on them. That means thirty-six Bread cards, four Pepper cards, five Mead cards, and three Silk cards. Remove all of these and put them back in the box. If you are playing with four or five players, skip this step and keep the full deck.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Goods Deck
walkthrough.text: Now shuffle all the remaining goods cards into one big deck. There are two hundred sixteen cards in the full set, consisting of legal goods like Apples, Cheese, Bread, and Chicken, plus contraband like Pepper, Mead, Silk, and Crossbows. If you are using the Royal Goods module, shuffle those in too. Give the deck a thorough shuffle and place it face down in the center of the table.

walkthrough.title: Create the Discard Piles
walkthrough.text: From the top of the deck, reveal five cards face up and place them in a pile. This is discard pile one. Then reveal five more cards and create discard pile two. Place both piles where all players can see and examine them. These discard piles are always open information, and players will draw from them during the Market phase.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Merchant Stands
walkthrough.text: Give each player one Merchant Stand. This is the cardboard piece that represents your market stall. During the game, you will place goods you successfully smuggle past the Sheriff face up in your stand. Contraband that gets through goes face down at the top of your stand so other players cannot see its value until final scoring.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Merchant Bags
walkthrough.text: Give each player one snap-closure Merchant Bag. This is the cloth bag you will use every round to load your goods. You will place one to five cards inside, snap it shut, and hand it to the Sheriff. The bag hides your cards so the Sheriff has to decide whether to trust you or inspect.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Starting Gold
walkthrough.text: Choose one player to be the banker. The banker distributes fifty gold to each player from the bank. Make sure the banker keeps their personal coins separate from the bank supply. Gold is used for bribes, penalties, and endgame scoring. The bank holds one hundred ten total coins in denominations of one, five, twenty, and fifty.

walkthrough.title: Deal Starting Hands
walkthrough.text: Deal six cards face down to each player from the top of the goods deck. Players pick up their cards and look at them privately. Your hand is always hidden from other players. You will always have exactly six cards in hand at the start of each round's Market phase.

walkthrough.title: Select the First Sheriff
walkthrough.text: Now pick the first Sheriff. The official rule is that whoever has the most actual real-world cash on their person becomes the first Sheriff. If that feels awkward or there is a tie, just pick someone randomly. Give the Sheriff marker to the starting Sheriff.

walkthrough.title: Know the Endgame Trigger
walkthrough.text: Before you start, make sure everyone knows when the game ends. In a three-player game, the game ends after each player has been Sheriff three times. In a four or five-player game, it ends after each player has been Sheriff two times. Keep count as you go. Setup is complete. Let's learn the rules.

walkthrough.title: Round Overview
walkthrough.text: Each round of Sheriff of Nottingham has five phases that happen in a fixed order. First is the Market phase where merchants cycle cards. Second is Loading Bags where you secretly choose what to put in your bag. Third is Declaration where you tell the Sheriff what you are bringing. Fourth is Inspection where the Sheriff decides who to check. Fifth is End of Round where the Sheriff marker passes and hands are refilled. Let's walk through each phase.

walkthrough.title: Phase One: Market
walkthrough.text: Starting with the player to the Sheriff's left and going clockwise, each merchant may discard up to five cards from their hand and draw back up to six. You can draw from the top of either discard pile or from the face-down draw pile, one card at a time. If you take from a discard pile, you must finish drawing from discards before drawing from the deck. All cards you discard go face up onto one discard pile. The Sheriff does not participate in the Market phase.

walkthrough.title: Phase Two: Load Merchant Bags
walkthrough.text: Now each merchant secretly selects one to five cards from their hand and places them in their Merchant Bag. Snap the bag closed so nobody can see inside. You must put at least one card and no more than five. You cannot put zero or six. The Sheriff does not load a bag.

walkthrough.title: Phase Three: Declaration
walkthrough.text: Each merchant must now declare what is in their bag. You must state exactly how many cards are in the bag, and you must name exactly one type of legal good. For example, you might say three Apples or five Cheese. The number of cards must be truthful. The type of good can be a total lie. You are never allowed to declare contraband. Even if every card in your bag is contraband, you must name a legal good.

walkthrough.title: The Declaration Rule
walkthrough.text: Let's be very clear on what you can and cannot lie about. You must always tell the truth about the number of cards. If you say three, there must be exactly three cards in the bag. You may lie about the type. You can say three Apples when the bag actually contains two Pepper and one Mead. But you cannot say three when there are actually four. This rule is critical and new players often get it wrong.

walkthrough.title: Phase Four: Inspection and Negotiation
walkthrough.text: Now the Sheriff decides whether to inspect each merchant's bag. Before opening any bags, players can negotiate. Merchants can offer bribes to the Sheriff to leave their bag alone, or to inspect a rival's bag. Bribes can be gold, goods from your Merchant Stand, goods in your bag, or promises of future favors. However, cards still in your hand cannot be offered as bribes.

walkthrough.title: If the Bag Passes Unopened
walkthrough.text: If the Sheriff chooses not to inspect your bag, you open it and reveal the contents to everyone. Legal goods go face up in your Merchant Stand. Contraband goes face down at the top of your stand. If you offered a bribe, you pay it now. All goods make it through regardless of whether they match your declaration.

walkthrough.title: If the Sheriff Inspects: Truthful Merchant
walkthrough.text: If the Sheriff opens your bag and your declaration was completely truthful, meaning the type and count match exactly, the Sheriff must pay you a penalty. The penalty is the sum of the penalty values printed on each card in your bag. All your goods then go into your Merchant Stand as normal. Being truthful and getting inspected is profitable.

walkthrough.title: If the Sheriff Inspects: Lying Merchant
walkthrough.text: If the Sheriff opens your bag and finds any cards that do not match your declaration, those mismatched cards are confiscated and discarded. You must pay the Sheriff a penalty for each confiscated card based on its printed penalty value. Any cards that did match your declaration still go into your Merchant Stand. Getting caught lying is expensive.

walkthrough.title: Bribe Rules and Deal Binding
walkthrough.text: Let's clarify bribes. Deals are only binding if they can be completed in the current round. If you promise to pay someone gold this round, you must do it. But promises about future rounds are not binding. You can promise to go easy on someone next round when you are Sheriff, and then break that promise with no consequences. Be careful who you trust.

walkthrough.title: Debt and Shortage Handling
walkthrough.text: If you owe gold and cannot pay, you must pay with legal goods from your Merchant Stand instead. The value of goods used must equal or exceed the debt, and there is no change given back. If your legal goods are not enough, you must reveal and pay with face-down contraband. If your stand is completely exhausted, any remaining debt is considered paid.

walkthrough.title: Phase Five: End of Round
walkthrough.text: After all bags are resolved, the Sheriff marker passes to the left. All players draw back up to six cards from the deck if they have fewer. The new Sheriff sits out the next Market, Loading, and Declaration phases while the other players cycle cards and prepare bags. A new round begins.

walkthrough.title: Game End
walkthrough.text: The game ends when each player has been Sheriff the required number of times: three times each in a three-player game, or two times each with four or five players. When the last Sheriff round is complete, proceed to final scoring.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: At game end, all players discard any cards remaining in their hands. They score zero. Then everyone reveals their face-down contraband in their Merchant Stands. Your score is the total of: all gold in your personal stash, the printed value of all legal goods in your stand, and the printed value of all contraband and Royal Goods in your stand.

walkthrough.title: King and Queen Bonuses
walkthrough.text: After base scoring, award King and Queen bonuses for each of the four legal good types: Apples, Cheese, Bread, and Chicken. For each type, the player with the most of that good in their stand earns the King bonus. The player with the second most earns the Queen bonus. If you are using Royal Goods, they count as two or three copies of their matching legal good for these majority races. King and Queen bonuses can swing the game significantly.

walkthrough.title: Winning the Game
walkthrough.text: Add King and Queen bonuses to your base score. The player with the highest total wins. If tied, the player with the most legal goods wins. If still tied, the player with the most contraband wins. If somehow still tied, you share the victory. A typical winning score ranges from one hundred fifty to two hundred fifty depending on player count and playstyle.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Market
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Sheriff of Nottingham! In this practice tutorial, we will walk through the first two rounds of a four-player game. Player 1 is the starting Sheriff. Players 2, 3, and 4 are merchants trying to get goods past the Sheriff. You will see honest plays, bluffs, mistakes, and the lessons they teach. Ready? Let's open for business.

walkthrough.title: Round One: Market Phase
walkthrough.text: Player 1 is the Sheriff this round, so they sit out the Market phase. Players 2, 3, and 4 look at their six-card hands. Player 2 has three Apples, one Cheese, one Pepper, and one Mead. Player 3 has two Cheese, two Bread, one Chicken, and one Silk. Player 4 has four Apples and two Crossbows. Each merchant now decides which cards to keep and which to discard and replace.

walkthrough.title: Player 2: Market Strategy
walkthrough.text: Player 2 discards the Pepper and Mead to a discard pile, then draws two cards from the face-down deck. They draw another Apple and a Chicken. Now Player 2 has four Apples, one Cheese, and one Chicken. A nice, mostly honest hand with a strong Apple base.

walkthrough.title: Player 3: Market Strategy
walkthrough.text: Player 3 sees a Mead sitting in the discard pile and grabs it. Remember, discard pile cards are open information, so everyone notices Player 3 took a contraband card. Player 3 draws one more from the deck. Now they have two Cheese, two Bread, one Chicken, one Silk, and one Mead, but only six cards total. They now have some interesting options.

walkthrough.title: Loading Bags
walkthrough.text: Now all merchants secretly load their bags. Player 2 puts four Apples in the bag. Totally honest. Player 3 puts two Cheese and one Silk in the bag. That is a gamble, mixing legal and contraband. Player 4 puts three Apples and two Crossbows in the bag. Five cards, and Player 4 is going to try to smuggle those Crossbows through. All bags snap shut.

walkthrough.title: Declaration Time
walkthrough.text: Each merchant declares. Player 2 says four Apples. Player 3 says three Cheese. Player 4 says five Apples. Remember, the count must be truthful, but the goods type can be a lie. Player 3 actually has two Cheese and one Silk, so they lied about the type. Player 4 has three Apples and two Crossbows, so they also lied about the type.

walkthrough.title: The Sheriff Decides
walkthrough.text: Player 1, the Sheriff, now looks at each declaration and decides who to inspect. Player 2 said four Apples. That is a big number, but Player 2 did not take any suspicious cards from the discard pile. Player 3 said three Cheese, but everyone saw them grab a Mead from the discard pile. Player 4 said five Apples, which is the maximum bag size and might be suspicious.

walkthrough.title: Player 4's Mistake: Overloading the Bag
walkthrough.text: Player 4 declared five Apples, the maximum. Declaring the maximum number of cards is a red flag for experienced players because it suggests you are trying to sneak as much through as possible. The Sheriff decides to inspect Player 4's bag. Inside: three Apples and two Crossbows. Player 4 must pay the penalty on both Crossbows, which is a hefty amount. The Apples go to their stand, but the Crossbows are confiscated and discarded.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Don't Overload with Contraband
walkthrough.text: Loading the maximum five cards when some are contraband is a common beginner mistake. It draws attention because honest merchants rarely need to ship five goods at once. A subtler approach is to mix one contraband card with two or three legal goods. Smaller bags attract less scrutiny, and if caught, the penalty is smaller.

walkthrough.title: Negotiation in Action
walkthrough.text: Before the Sheriff decides about Player 3, negotiation breaks out. Player 3 offers five gold for the Sheriff to leave their bag alone. Player 2 says nothing, hoping to seem innocent. The Sheriff considers: Player 3 took a Mead from the discard pile, so they are probably lying. But five gold is five gold. The Sheriff takes the bribe and lets Player 3's bag through.

walkthrough.title: Player 3 Gets Away With It
walkthrough.text: Player 3 opens their bag: two Cheese and one Silk. The Cheese cards go face up in the stand, and the Silk goes face down. Player 3 smuggled contraband through successfully, minus the five-gold bribe. Meanwhile, Player 2's bag passes without inspection. Four Apples go into their stand, building toward the Apple King bonus.

walkthrough.title: Round Two: New Sheriff
walkthrough.text: The Sheriff marker passes left to Player 2. Now Player 2 sits out while Players 1, 3, and 4 are merchants. Everyone draws back up to six cards. A new round begins with fresh Market, Loading, and Declaration phases.

walkthrough.title: Player 3's Mistake: Greedy Repeat
walkthrough.text: Emboldened by last round's success, Player 3 tries to smuggle again. They load two Mead and one Pepper into the bag and declare three Chicken. But Player 2, the new Sheriff, remembers that Player 3 smuggled last round. Player 2 inspects. All three cards are contraband. Player 3 pays penalties on all three cards and gets nothing into their stand. The bribe profit from last round is wiped out and then some.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Vary Your Strategy
walkthrough.text: Smuggling twice in a row is risky because other players remember what happened last round. If you pulled off a bluff, send an honest bag next time to rebuild credibility. The best merchants alternate between honest rounds and sneaky ones, keeping the Sheriff guessing. Predictability is your enemy.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Honest Shipping
walkthrough.text: Player 1, now a merchant, loads three Bread into their bag and declares three Bread. Completely honest. The new Sheriff, Player 2, inspects anyway because they are cautious. The bag matches the declaration perfectly. Player 2 must pay Player 1 the penalty value of all three Bread cards. Being truthful and getting inspected is profitable. Player 1 earns goods in their stand and gold from the penalty.

walkthrough.title: Player 4's Mistake: Bad Bribe Math
walkthrough.text: Player 4 offers twenty gold to the Sheriff to let their bag through. Their bag contains two Apples and one Chicken, all legal. The total value of those three cards is about nine gold in goods value. Player 4 just paid twenty gold to avoid an inspection they would have passed anyway. Even if they were smuggling, twenty gold is often too much to pay for the cargo's value.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Calculate Your Bribe Value
walkthrough.text: Never offer a bribe worth more than the cargo you are protecting. If your bag contains ten gold worth of contraband, a bribe of five gold makes sense because you profit five gold net. But a bribe of twenty gold on a bag worth nine gold is throwing money away. Before offering, quickly estimate the penalty you would pay if caught versus the bribe amount. Bribes should protect profit, not create losses.

walkthrough.title: Mid-Game Strategy Check
walkthrough.text: Let's look at the standings. Player 2 has four Apples in their stand, leading the Apple race. Player 1 has three Bread, leading Bread. Player 3 has two Cheese and one face-down Silk but took a hit from failed smuggling. Player 4 has three Apples and is burning through gold. The King and Queen bonuses will be decided by who accumulates the most of each legal good by game end.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations, you have walked through the core mechanics of Sheriff of Nottingham! You have seen how the Market, Declaration, Inspection, and Negotiation phases work. You have watched three common mistakes: overloading with contraband, smuggling twice in a row, and overbribing. The key to this game is reading people, mixing honest and sneaky plays, and knowing when a bribe is worth it.

walkthrough.title: Before You Open for Business
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for bluffing basics and Sheriff strategy.

Second, reference the Appendix for card values, penalty amounts, and King/Queen bonus details.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: Before You Open for Business
bullets: Check out General Tips → bluffing and Sheriff strategy
Reference the Appendix → card values, penalties, King/Queen bonuses
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Establish Credibility Early
walkthrough.text: In the first round or two, consider sending completely honest bags. When the Sheriff inspects and finds you were truthful, two good things happen. First, you earn penalty gold from the Sheriff. Second, you build a reputation as an honest merchant. That reputation pays dividends later when you actually want to smuggle. The table will remember that you were honest early and give you the benefit of the doubt.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Discard Piles
walkthrough.text: Pay close attention to what other merchants take from the discard piles during the Market phase. If someone grabs a Crossbow or Mead from the discard pile, everyone knows they have contraband. This information is gold for the Sheriff. As a merchant, be careful about taking visible contraband. Sometimes it is better to draw blind from the deck even if a tempting contraband card is sitting in the discard.

walkthrough.title: Mix Legal and Contraband Smartly
walkthrough.text: The safest way to smuggle is to hide one contraband card among two or three legal goods that match your declaration. If you declare three Apples and the bag actually contains two Apples and one Pepper, getting caught only costs you the penalty on one card. The two Apples still go into your stand. This limits your downside while giving you smuggling upside.

walkthrough.title: Sheriff Strategy: Don't Over-Inspect
walkthrough.text: As Sheriff, resist the urge to inspect every bag. Every time you open a truthful bag, you pay the merchant a penalty. If you inspect three bags and two are honest, you have paid out significant gold while only catching one liar. Focus your inspections on the most suspicious merchants and let the others through. Sometimes the best Sheriff move is to collect bribes and save your gold.

walkthrough.title: Focus on One or Two Legal Goods
walkthrough.text: The King and Queen bonuses for each legal good type can be worth twenty or more points. Choose one or two legal goods to specialize in and accumulate them aggressively. Apples are the most plentiful, making the Apple race the most competitive. Chicken is the scarcest, so fewer cards are needed to win that King bonus. Pick your lane and ship those goods consistently.

walkthrough.title: Manage Your Gold Carefully
walkthrough.text: Gold is both a resource and a scoring component. Every coin you spend on bribes or lose to penalties is a coin you do not have at game end. Budget your gold carefully. A good rule of thumb: never spend more than one third of your gold on a single bribe or risk. Keep a reserve of at least twenty gold for flexibility in later rounds.

walkthrough.title: Read the Table, Not Just the Cards
walkthrough.text: Sheriff of Nottingham is a social game. Pay attention to body language, voice tone, and behavioral patterns. Does a player always fidget when they are lying? Does someone always offer bribes only when smuggling? The cards matter, but the people matter more. The best Sheriffs catch liars by reading faces, and the best merchants control their tells.

walkthrough.title: Know When to Play It Safe
walkthrough.text: Not every round needs to be a high-stakes gamble. Sometimes the smartest play is three honest Apples in the bag, building your King bonus while keeping your gold intact. The players who win consistently are not the ones who pull off the biggest bluffs. They are the ones who choose the right moment to bluff and play it safe the rest of the time.

summary.title: Establish Credibility Early
bullets: Send honest bags in rounds 1-2 → build truthful reputation
Inspected truthful bags earn you penalty gold from Sheriff
Reputation pays dividends when you actually want to smuggle later

summary.title: Watch the Discard Piles
bullets: Discard pile draws are visible — everyone sees what you take
Taking visible contraband creates instant suspicion
Sometimes draw blind from deck to hide your intentions

summary.title: Mix Legal and Contraband Smartly
bullets: Hide 1 contraband among 2-3 matching legal goods
If caught, only pay penalty on 1 card — legal goods still score
Limits downside while maintaining smuggling upside

summary.title: Sheriff Strategy: Don't Over-Inspect
bullets: Opening truthful bags costs you penalty gold
Focus inspections on most suspicious merchants
Sometimes collecting bribes > catching liars

summary.title: Focus on One or Two Legal Goods
bullets: King/Queen bonuses worth 20+ pts per good type
Apples most plentiful = most competitive race
Chicken scarcest = fewer cards needed for King bonus

summary.title: Manage Your Gold Carefully
bullets: Gold = resource AND endgame scoring
Don't spend more than 1/3 of gold on a single bribe/risk
Keep 20+ gold reserve for late-game flexibility

summary.title: Read the Table, Not Just the Cards
bullets: Watch body language, voice tone, behavioral patterns
Cards matter but people matter more
Best Sheriffs catch liars by reading faces, not just math

summary.title: Know When to Play It Safe
bullets: Not every round needs to be high-stakes
3 honest Apples builds King bonus + preserves gold
Win by choosing the right moment to bluff, not bluffing every round

walkthrough.title: Reputation Management
walkthrough.text: Advanced players think of their reputation as a resource. Start honest to build trust, then cash in that trust with a well-timed smuggle. After a successful bluff, immediately return to honest shipping for a round or two. The rhythm is honest, honest, bluff, honest, bluff. Each honest round makes the next bluff more believable. Players who bluff every round get inspected every round and bleed gold.

walkthrough.title: Sheriff Economic Model
walkthrough.text: As Sheriff, think in expected value terms. If you believe a merchant is lying fifty percent of the time, and the average penalty is eight gold, inspecting has an expected value of four gold minus the penalty you pay if they are honest. Calculate whether the expected inspection return exceeds the cost of being wrong. In many cases, accepting a small bribe of three to five gold is a guaranteed return that beats the gamble of inspection.

walkthrough.title: Targeted King and Queen Racing
walkthrough.text: The King and Queen bonuses are often the deciding factor in close games. Track what legal goods each opponent is accumulating in their stand. If someone has six Apples and you have four, you might need to pivot to a less contested good type. Cheese and Bread King bonuses are often cheaper to win because fewer players compete for them. In the late game, a single shipment of three Bread can swing the King bonus your way.

walkthrough.title: Contraband Timing
walkthrough.text: The best time to smuggle contraband is when you are Sheriff next round. Other players know you are about to become Sheriff, so they are less likely to antagonize you by inspecting your bag this round. They want to stay on your good side. Use this social dynamic to slip through high-value contraband like Crossbows or Silk right before your Sheriff turn.

walkthrough.title: Bribe Judo
walkthrough.text: Advanced negotiation uses bribes as information tools. If you are the Sheriff and a merchant offers a suspiciously large bribe, that merchant is probably smuggling high-value contraband. The bribe size often correlates with cargo value. Conversely, merchants can offer small bribes on honest bags to create a pattern, then offer the same small bribe when actually smuggling. The goal is to make your bribe pattern unreadable.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Calculation
walkthrough.text: In the final one or two rounds, shift to pure calculation. Count the goods in each stand, estimate gold reserves, and calculate exactly how many King and Queen bonuses you can win. If you are safely ahead in Apples, stop shipping Apples and focus on a second bonus race. If you cannot win any King bonus, maximize contraband value and gold conservation. The last rounds are about math, not bluffing.

walkthrough.title: Table Politics
walkthrough.text: Sheriff of Nottingham is deeply political. Use your Sheriff turns to build alliances. Let a player through who helped you last round. Inspect the player who is winning to slow them down. Encourage other players to contest the same King bonus as your rival. The social meta-game is as important as the card game. Players who manage relationships well gain subtle advantages that compound over the entire game.

walkthrough.title: Declaring the Perfect Number
walkthrough.text: Your declaration count sends a signal. One or two cards feels safe and boring but scores little. Four or five cards feels greedy and draws inspection. Three cards is the sweet spot. It is believable, hard to read, and still delivers meaningful goods if it passes. When in doubt, load three cards and declare three of whatever legal good you have the most of.

summary.title: Reputation Management
bullets: Rhythm: honest → honest → bluff → honest → bluff
Each honest round makes next bluff more believable
Bluff every round = inspected every round = gold bleed

summary.title: Sheriff Economic Model
bullets: Calculate expected value: (lie probability x penalty) - (truth probability x your penalty cost)
Small guaranteed bribe often beats risky inspection
Don't inspect unless suspicion justifies the gamble

summary.title: Targeted King and Queen Racing
bullets: Track opponents' stand contents throughout the game
Pivot to less contested goods if behind in main race
Cheese/Bread King often cheaper to win than Apples

summary.title: Contraband Timing
bullets: Smuggle the round before you become Sheriff
Others don't want to antagonize the next Sheriff
Social dynamic protects high-value contraband

summary.title: Bribe Judo
bullets: Large bribe = probably smuggling high-value cargo
Small consistent bribes create unreadable patterns
Use bribe amounts as information tools

summary.title: Endgame Calculation
bullets: Final 1-2 rounds: pure math, not bluffing
Count stand goods, estimate gold, calculate bonuses
Maximize weakest scoring area or protect existing leads

summary.title: Table Politics
bullets: Use Sheriff turns to build alliances and slow leaders
Let through players who helped you last round
Social meta-game compounds advantages over full game

summary.title: Declaring the Perfect Number
bullets: 1-2 cards: safe but low value; 4-5 cards: draws suspicion
3 cards: sweet spot — believable + meaningful value
When in doubt, load 3 of your most common legal good

entries.definition: Cards that can be honestly declared: Apples, Cheese, Bread, and Chicken. Placed face up in your Merchant Stand when they pass through. Each has a point value used for scoring.
Cards that cannot be declared: Pepper, Mead, Silk, and Crossbow. Must be smuggled through by lying about bag contents. Placed face down in stand if successful. Higher point values than legal goods.

Optional cards that count as contraband during play but count as 2-3 copies of a matching legal good for King/Queen bonus races at scoring. Examples: Royal Cheddar, Gouda Cheese, Rye Bread, Golden Apple.
Legal good. 48 cards in full deck. Worth 2 gold each. Penalty value: 2. The most plentiful legal good, making the Apple King race the most competitive.
Legal good. 36 cards in full deck. Worth 3 gold each. Penalty value: 2. Mid-tier quantity and value.
Legal good. 36 cards in full deck (36 removed in 3-player). Worth 3 gold each. Penalty value: 2. Mid-tier quantity and value.
Legal good. 24 cards in full deck. Worth 4 gold each. Penalty value: 2. The scarcest legal good, making Chicken King easier to win with fewer cards.
Contraband. 22 cards in full deck. Worth 6 gold each. Penalty value: 6. Most common contraband type.
Contraband. 21 cards in full deck. Worth 7 gold each. Penalty value: 7. High-value smuggling target.
Contraband. 12 cards in full deck. Worth 8 gold each. Penalty value: 8. Scarce but very valuable.
Contraband. 5 cards in full deck. Worth 9 gold each. Penalty value: 9. Highest value and highest risk contraband.
Your personal market stall. Legal goods are placed face up; contraband face down. Stand contents are scored at game end. Face-down cards are revealed during final scoring.
Snap-closure cloth bag used to secretly transport 1-5 cards each round. Conceals card contents from the Sheriff until inspected or passed through.
Token indicating the current Sheriff. Passes left each round. The Sheriff sits out Market, Loading, and Declaration phases but decides all inspections.
First phase of each round. Merchants (not Sheriff) may discard up to 5 cards and draw back to 6 from discard piles or the deck. Discard draws must precede deck draws.
Second phase. Each merchant secretly places 1-5 cards in their bag and snaps it closed. Zero or 6+ cards is illegal.
Third phase. Each merchant states the exact card count (must be truthful) and exactly one legal good type (may lie). Cannot declare contraband.
Fourth phase. Sheriff decides which bags to open, with negotiation allowed before each decision. Outcomes depend on whether declaration was truthful or false.
Offer made to the Sheriff to influence inspection decisions. Can be gold, stand goods, bag goods, or same-round favors. Hand cards cannot be offered. Deals are binding only for the current round.
When bag contents exactly match the declared type and count. If inspected while truthful, the Sheriff pays the merchant the penalty value of all cards in the bag.

When bag contents do not match the declared type. If inspected, mismatched cards are confiscated and the merchant pays the Sheriff their penalty values. Cards matching the declaration still go to the stand.

The gold amount printed on each card representing the cost of getting caught. Paid by the lying merchant to the Sheriff, or by the Sheriff to a truthful merchant. Higher for contraband than legal goods.

End-of-game bonus awarded to the player with the most of each legal good type in their stand. One King bonus per good type (Apples, Cheese, Bread, Chicken). Worth 20 points for Apples, and varying amounts for others.
End-of-game bonus awarded to the player with the second-most of each legal good type. Smaller than King bonus but still significant.
When the Sheriff inspects and finds cards not matching the declaration, those cards are discarded to a discard pile. The Sheriff chooses the discard order. Confiscated cards score zero.
Rule governing deal enforcement. Deals are binding only if completable within the current round. Future-round promises (like going easy as Sheriff next round) are not enforceable.
In a 3-player game, remove all cards marked with the 4+ icon before shuffling: 36 Bread, 4 Pepper, 5 Mead, 3 Silk. This tightens the deck for fewer players.
The game ends after a specific number of Sheriff rotations. 3 players: each player is Sheriff 3 times. 4-5 players: each player is Sheriff 2 times.

If a player cannot pay a gold penalty, they pay with legal goods from their stand (value must meet or exceed debt, no change). If legal goods are insufficient, contraband is revealed and used. If stand is empty, debt is forgiven.
